---
title: "2.12 Informal Run-Time Analysis"
subtitle: "Selection and Iteration"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Loop Analysis

### Run-time analysis begins with careful tracing

This topic is not formal Big-O notation.  
It is about estimating <span class="term">how many times code runs</span>.

## Tracing Complex Loops

### Multiple variables must be tracked together

![Loop trace table](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-12-informal-run-time-analysis/whileLoopTrace.png){fig-align="center" width="24%"}

When analyzing a loop, students should track:

::: {.tight-list}
- iteration number
- variable updates
- loop condition checks
- output produced
:::

Short-circuit behavior can also affect whether part of a condition is evaluated.

## Counting Loop Iterations

### A loop's run count can often be computed directly

For simple counted loops:

::: {.tight-list}
- if the loop uses `counter <= limit`, the largest running value is `limit`
- if the loop uses `counter < limit`, the largest running value is `limit - 1`
- an informal count can be computed as `largest - smallest + 1`
:::

This is the basic classroom rule for quick iteration counts.

## Analyzing Nested Loops

### Rectangular nested loops multiply counts

```java
for (int row = 0; row < 5; row++)
{
    for (int col = 0; col < 10; col++)
    {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
}
```

::: {.tight-list}
- outer loop runs 5 times
- inner loop runs 10 times per outer pass
- total inner-body executions: `5 * 10 = 50`
:::

## Non-Rectangular Nested Loops

### When the inner limit depends on the outer variable, the count changes each row

```java
for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++)
{
    for (int j = 1; j <= i; j++)
    {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
}
```

This creates a triangle pattern rather than a rectangle because the inner loop length grows with the outer loop.

![Gauss summation idea](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-12-informal-run-time-analysis/sumFormula.png){fig-align="center" width="44%"}

For this classroom level, the useful takeaway is that the total can be found by adding `1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n`, or by using the formula `n(n+1)/2`.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- tracing loops with multiple changing variables
- counting stars and loop executions in simple loops
- multiplying counts in rectangular nested loops
- summing counts in non-rectangular nested loops
- <span class="term">2.12.3 Coding Challenge: POGIL Analyzing Loops</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- trace loop conditions and variable updates accurately
- count iterations in simple counted loops
- multiply outer and inner counts for rectangular nested loops
- explain why non-rectangular nested loops do not use one fixed inner count
- connect triangular loop counts to `1 + 2 + ... + n`
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
